speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: I found a little picture this morning going to a drawer, I got the drawers in there, and a picture of my sister and myself. And that was taken in Missouri just before we left and that was about the same time that the captain was taken. But it's... I think I got a hold of it pretty easily. I think it was Missouri that I was just about... I think it must have been about six there and seven here. Because it looks a little bit older than that does. And they were both made in Missouri.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: How'd you come to meet with her?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Well, Dad had always kind of had a hankering to come out west. He wanted to know what this west was all about. So that's a good show.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: That's a couple of my boys when they were little. That's the second and the younger one. Buster Brown's too. Yes, Buster Brown's all the way. That one is the one in the kitchen now. Wayne and the other one is Lloyd. But now the boys are wearing their hair like that now. And this way.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Oh yeah, he always kind of had a hand cream to know what all this west was like. He wanted to come out that way and he'd like to go out west, he thought. So Mother wasn't very anxious about the idea. Well, she didn't kick so much, except he finally got a chance to go out. You know for a fellow that's just working a little bit of time here and there. Our jobs, you don't have money enough to trip on them very often. So there's a friend of his there shipping a carload of cattle out there. I don't know if he's going to Denver or whether he's going to Colorado or Wyoming. Anyhow, Dad was supposed to go along with that car of cattle and care for them. They had butter and food and things along their trip. He was all up in the air that he got a chance to go. Mother didn't want him to go very bad. She just assumed he was going to stay out of that. Kind of a mess, she thought.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: How long was he going to be gone?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Well, he was just going to go out there. And I think he had the idea that if he gets to work out there, he would stay out there and work. Well, he got out there and did all right on his trip. And then I got work right away on the railroad. There was a wonderful man that run the railroad. He got a clean with him. It was Miss Lee. That's what his name was.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Anyhow, he was the foreman of that carpenter gang. Dad was on the carpenter building bridges, the ridge gang. So he got to work there and wrote to the back mothers. He had gotten their job. He said he'd work there for, you know, they got a little stake ahead and they'd send for us. And so, the mother there didn't get to go. She'd been getting killed just before, after he had left. My aunt was, one of her younger sisters, was to be married soon. She was having a big wedding. It was at the home, but... They had this big wedding with the, well, they were sewing on her dress and all the fancy frills that were to go on it. They ran out of lace to finish it up. So, her name was Chloe, the one that was to get married. But mother, her name was Amy, and her younger sister was Lenora. Leora. No, it doesn't. It's Lenora. And they went to town one morning, hitched up the buggy. Team, little team. They always, no town was anything they needed. They lived five, six miles out, I guess. And they went down. They were just a little visit inside of the house. It must have been about a block or so from our house. And they would go right along the edge of the fence, and they had to go down a little dip, across a little ditch that led down to bridges over the creek. And the boys had trimmed up the orchard. And I had to put all the trimmings of the orchard, threw it over in that creek down there. I don't know how come, but that's where they were right now. And they went over this way, then down, across the bridge, and then go up again, and go on the other way. Well, when they got down here and across the bridge, you know it, and they started up there again, the, what was it, the neck duke, or the, whatever, the front part of the harness goes across them here. And that come and hitched somewhere, or something broke about it, and turned the buggy loose, and it just rolled right down the bridge, and off over the side, and dumped them right out of that buggy, and into that bunch of the willows, I mean of the limbs and prunings. And they thought her neck was broken for a while. And there was a stick run right into her ear, and punctured the ear down. One of those, on those limbs, a little stick, and she was unconscious for two, three, four days there at the home. And they just, the other one, the sister came running back in, and it didn't hurt her. And she, they came running back in for help, and I guess one of them went for the doctor, and the other, and she brought her on in the house. And she was unconscious for several days, and she finally come out of it though, and, but she never could hear any of that one ear. So, that was kind of an offset. But she, I guess was able to, I don't remember about that wedding, maybe we left before the wedding, I don't know, but I don't think we did, because that wasn't very long. Until the wedding, I don't think, but they were doing all this rushing and fussing around on.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: So, that, Dad worked there for a while, until, well, he figured he had a paycheck or two. And he was, I think he had to work a month or something like that before he was entitled to a pass on the railroad. As soon as he'd filled in his time, he sent the pass back and said, come on.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: How did your mom feel about it?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Oh, well, she felt all right. She'd kind of gotten off it, she'd go try it and see what it was all like, you know. So, we went out and landed in Laramie, Wyoming.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Did you work for them?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: I just, I wanted to be a friend of the folks, I wasn't old enough to think it made much difference, although I suppose we had, I don't remember the dating at all. So, that's kind of skipped, that's all skipped, I don't remember anything about that. But I suppose, kid -like, we kind of hated it, to leave grandmother and all those folks. Because there was eight of the kids, they were all grown up. At that time, all grown, and grandmother and grandfather. But then, still, I guess I was anxious to see dad, too. It wasn't, I guess, making much difference to me at that time.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Do you remember the train ride out?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: No, I guess I don't do that either. Down to Laramie, but after we lived in Laramie a while, I remember parts of the town. I remember we lived just about two blocks from the university there. And I remember the girl that went, it was a Wechter girl, that was her first name, but her last name was Wechter. And so, Mother asked her, she admitted, she asked her if she'd care to take me over to a school. She went to school the next day, and she said yes, she did. She said she didn't want me to go alone, and she wanted the girl to look after me, I was on the way there and back. I guess it wasn't very far to school, but it was a strange place to me, you know. So I went to school with her, and got used to it. She'd always come home to her meal, they'd just live right across the street from us. So that was handy.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: You didn't stay there too long, did you?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: I was, I think, promoted to the third grade, I would say. I went to school there, and of course they started me in the first grade, but I had gone with my aunt to school. I had one aunt that was the youngest of the whole bunch of them, and she was still going to school. She was grown up, she was, I suppose, 15, 16 years old. And she would take me to school with her once in a while.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Partly she thought it would be good for me to learn how school went on and stuff like that. And I think at this time I was beginning to learn my ABCs and things of that sort, you know. I just remember them kind of talking about this time I was in school. It was, I think, about a mile to go, or something like that. So I didn't go much of time with bad weather or anything. Some days I'd go and some days I didn't. Whatever they said, it was a bad day, they just kept me home. They'd get me to take me to school, and I learned, I was learning these little words to read, you know. I think I already knew my ABCs and things. I could count and I didn't know them at all. So finally the teacher would take me on his lap, and talk to me about the lesson and teach me. And I was awfully embarrassed because he thought I was a baby and I just wasn't. I was kind of, I didn't say anything, but I kind of wished that I didn't have to sit on his lap. He just thought he was being real nice to me. I guess I was a pet of the whole bunch, because I was young and something around there. And I knew in the school. So I was reading those little things that they do. I could do pretty good for, I guess I could do part of that at home. I think I probably knew my ABCs before that. But anyhow, I was learning then. They said, I know they thought I was learning fast enough, I wasn't losing anything. But anyhow, in Missouri that's the first hint of school I had had. And when they got to learn me, they put me there in the first grade. And I was in there two weeks, and they sent me over to second grade. So I guess I was getting along real fast. And I was learning that real fast. And when they had the music, the vocal lessons in there and all that. And the teacher went and told, I can remember just parts of that. I remember them going and talking to the other teacher. I remember the kids in the room, the two rooms. That's all clear to me. But she told them, she said, I think this little girl she said is ahead of my grades. And she said, I think she needs to be in the other class. I think she was able to handle it somehow. They put her over there and tried her. And she told them that the music I felt it had. They put me in there in the second grade. And at the same time, I ate that up. I just figured there was some time for the music. And I did have it. You do, you know, training, voice training. And then they'd pick up certain notes and sound a certain, and I could do it. And the other kids, it wasn't hardly any of the kids, do it readily. They'd have to make an effort to it or a second or so. And I could run the scale upwards or backwards or whichever way you wanted. So they kept me right on that. So I was, I think I was, I remember right, I was getting ready for third grade when I left there. I was promoted. Then we left on it in the other meeting. I don't know. No, I don't know. I guess I do too.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: There was a, Bobbinson was that. I knew that didn't sound like the name that the bridge foreman, that boss. His name was Robins. Instead of Lee. The next people got acquainted with, were in Rexburg. They were Lee. I knew that didn't sound right. I knew that it was Lee concerned. That is S .G. Robinson, I think it was. And had a little boy about five years old. And his name was Stillman Robinson. I'm not sure he said it. Stillman something or other. Robinson.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: After we'd been there a while, and Dad was still working with the bridge gang. Mrs. Robinson, she was there on the, well she cooked for the men, you know. They had the cars, lived in the cars, and they were all fixed up. They were cozy and that. But she had a hired girl.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: I think she was a Swedish girl. I remember right. Anyhow, she was a good worker and she was helping Robinson with all the work. Mrs. Robinson, a little bit of a whiff of the bees, nothing.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: So, I wanted to know of Dad. I said, why don't you bring your wife out sometime. I said, bring her out to visit us. She got kind of lonely there and there was no chance of her getting away. Visiting anybody. And she knew that we hadn't gotten a train and didn't know many people yet. So, he said, well you weren't trying to do that. It was all arranged. She'd be able to go out and spend Christmas with her. I was there. Everything was all set. Dad had been home the weekend before and he had made arrangements. Just how we were doing. And the regular line, the train came along here, but they were putting in a branch line into another place. And it wasn't about a quarter of a mile, I think, between that one and this one. He told them to have them let us, he told us to have the conductor let us off here at this crossing here. And about a quarter of a mile from the other one. He said they'd stop if you tell them that. And so he gave all instructions. So we got all ready. And it was winter time. Well, it was Christmas. Anyhow. So we were all outfitted for winter time. We got off of the train here and he put my sister in. And you see how big she was, this little picture. And she was light, too. She was always in the puny thing. And I was a husky. Nothing ever bothered me. I was always hard to, you know, nothing seemed to hurt me. And I'd get them everything that came along after I got out where I could. Now in the, before we left Missouri. I was about six then when we had the whooping cough. And I don't know who got it first, but anyhow, Edna, my sister, she was just coughing, just coughing her little head off. And it just cost her she'd fall over or something. She got used to it. As soon as she got started coughing, she'd run and grab for a chair and hold on to it. And tell mother to get a hold of her. And the doctor sometimes had to come a time or two during the day. And bring her every day. He was there for about two, three weeks. And most of the time he was just running over there. He lived right close to us. But they didn't know whether she'd go through or hardly. She just got her down and everything. And I didn't, I never did hoop. Mother said there's decent hoops that I'd cough time or two or just kind of come and cough. And there wasn't any hoop to it. And that's just the way everything went. After we got out west, they had, I went to school and got the chicken fox and went home, took them to her. And she just about croaked with them. She just splattered with them. And I had just about half a dozen spots I guess is all I had on me. Just all things like that. She always got the bed end of the deal. But when we went on this Christmas vacation, I had the conductor let us off on this.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: You had the water in the kitchen? Huh? You had the water in the kitchen?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Yes, in the bathroom too. And he let us off at that crossing. And put Edna off first. And there was wind like we had here the other day. And she, you know, the track was up high and then sloped down to the ditch thing. Well, Edna just went rolling right down and over me. The wind was strong. You pulled her right up the hill. And the train stopped by. She just rolled right along, you know, kind of toward the wheels. But she was going toward the ditch, you know. And then by that time, she'd gotten Mother off. And she dashed over between her and the conductor and got Edna picked up. And she hung on to her and then they put me off next. And I don't know, I don't remember what happened. I suppose Mother grabbed on to me kind of. But I could pretty well hold myself. After the train pulled out, we sat down right on the track there and held to the rails. And kept from blowing.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: And we had sat there quite a while. Dad was going to meet us when that train went through. But he was still pounding out of his carbon rain ever there. And he was still working away at it and missed a time. Didn't know what the trainers do yet. And I can't think of that girl's name. The Swedish name. And I don't know if it wasn't Hannah, but I guess I'll call her that for convenience. But she happened to be standing at the sink, washing dishes. And when she looked out the window, she says, Oh, Mrs. Robinson, look over there. Is that Mrs.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Woodson over there?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: And they looked out there and there we sat, you know, holding on to the track. She said, Yes, for heaven's sake. And she said, I'll go over and help. And Mrs. Robinson said, I'll go get Mr. Woodson. And I'll go tell him. And so here came that girl. The name just slips me each time. Anyhow, as I said, I'd call her Hannah. And she came running. And the wind just blew in every direction. You could hardly run against it or walk even. But she was used to it. And she came dashing over there to where we were. And she got us. But that time, the wind had whipped our hats off and our, we just pretty near stripped. We hadn't had our coats buttoned tight. And we all had new outfits. And we had a paycheck or two. And we'd gotten a new, Mother got her a new jacket. They wore those short jackets at that time. Fitted. And Mother had her new brown jacket and her brown hat. And it was all feathered and plumed. I don't know. It was all matched, you know, kind of nice. And she outfitted me in a red coat, the jacket and the red hat. And because of being so windy there sometimes, we had veils too. And tied them on. And Edna had done these little poke bonnets like they put on little kids, you know. And it was heavy. It was wool. A wooly thing. And her blue coat. So they were all fixed up nice. And they had mittens and everything. And rubbers and blushes. Anyhow, when we got off of there, and the wind got at us, first the veil would go here and the veil would go the other way. And then the hats went. Of course they weren't pinned on us, but the rubber band under the chin. And, well, the time we had the girl led us,

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: that strikes me that the girl's name was Sophie. I don't know. But anyhow, by the time we started to cross the meadow there to go over to the other place, by the...

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: We had lots of rubbers, or one of them, I remember, one rubber. And we had our pockets stuffed with candy and gum to take to a little boy, Stillman. And Edna just whipped the candy and stuff out of our pockets. And I don't know, I think one of the rubbers was gone. Or else one of us did have them and the other one didn't have them. But I think just one rubber is all that it left me with, I think. And I don't remember much about it, but I know our mittens were gone. And it just whipped the dickens right out of us pretty much. And so the time we had started back, toward the house, and Dad coming, and he was coming on the run. And at that time, we told him how it had happened, and he let her then take us over to the house. And he started up across the track and up over the hill on the other side of the track through the sagebrush. And he thought maybe he'd save some of our wraps and hats and coats and things, or mittens and things. And so he'd take out and get them, maybe catch them before they got too far, cause of the brush. He didn't get a thing. Not a thing. He'd retrieve. So, but he came on back then, and he was feeling better thinking he wasn't there when the train came through. There might have been a few things that we might have saved, but I don't think it very much, because you didn't dare let loose of that rail. Might have lost some water too. Yes, didn't dare let loose of that rail, or it'd blow you right on.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Did you have a good Christmas in the world?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Oh, I had an awful nice Christmas. Wonderful people. It was a good time. I never had had a red coat or hat or red dress or anything. I don't think I had red dress at that time, because my hair was so near red that they just didn't dress me in red. They just didn't look good. And so, but I had that, I had a deep dark red. It was an expensive coat and hat. And that was the first hat I'd had. I just felt, I felt real dressed up. And then I felt I'd lost a whole lot.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: We stayed, I don't know, several days out there, I think, and then went back.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Was any of that fun to Rexburg?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: It was a couple years ago. Well, it was, let's see.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: I have cancer, we're in Asia exactly, but we went to Rexburg. I don't know how we'd come to go over there. I know that there's smallpox in the country, in the Laramie there. A bunch of them came down, they were afraid that men were going to come down with it on the bridge gang. And so the order came down the line that everybody be vaccinated. And so there was a certain day set and the doctor was out there. Everybody comes to the car, their office car. And the doctor's sitting there and everybody comes with their sleeve rolled up. And so we got the dad and he had his sleeve rolled up and I said, go on.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: And he said, but didn't they run with him?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: I said, you don't need it. Because he tried to spot a scar about half a dollar. He had been vaccinated before and they said anybody with a scar like that don't need it. Don't need another one. So he was ready for it, but he didn't have to be vaccinated. But the rest of them all had a vaccination and went through. But I forgot to Rexburg. I don't know what the people that we met at Rexburg.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: So when it got quite a deal with the family with the name of Lee, I don't know what he did. He broke, I guess, away from home most of the time. I don't remember him. But Mrs. Lee, they had one grown -up boy, I think he was about 17 or 19 or something. And there was one about 15, I think it was him or two. And they lived over across the track from where we lived.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: We lived quite a little ways from the track, I would say, about a distance of maybe two blocks or three. And we used to go over to Lee's once in a while and maybe come to our place. We passed, to go to their house, we passed the home of Judge Ricks.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: He was a high -to -do Mormon at that time. I don't know what you call him. He was Judge Ricks anyhow. And I know that Ricks College I see is down there, I guess is named after him pretty much. But we passed their farm home there and he had, I don't know, four or five grown -up boys and some little kids. They had quite a big family. And then,

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: well, I used to play with it. This little girl got my age and so she and I used to play together quite often. Kids would come to my house and I'd go up to their place. Later on one day, well, he had his office in town, this judge, and he was club for it. So one Sunday he'd come down from, coming home, I mean he was coming from town, going out to his home. And he had just married a new wife. And one little girl came running back over to our house and said, Mama wants to know if she can borrow some, I don't know, salt or vanilla or something like that. But she wanted to, her mother asked her if they were going to have company or something going on. And she said, yes, Papa and Aunt, I don't know, Fanny or something are coming in. Mama's going to make some ice cream. And right after she's gone, Mother showed her hand and said, If she can make that other woman ice cream if she wants to, if it's me, I'd put on the teak, it'll go on her.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: I remembered hearing that, and I thought that was funny she'd say something like that. Because she thought after marrying a new young girl and bring her out there to visit her, after the second night to visit her, she just didn't approve of that at all. Mother listened.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Do you think your folks got along pretty well in that moment you were in here?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Yes, we got along fine. But Mother just kind of talked about that, that's why she sputtered about that, but she didn't say it to anybody else.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: So you finally didn't stay in West Virginia long enough?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Well, we moved to town, I think some moved around.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: I don't remember going to school there, but there was some place where we just had a little old Red Riding Hood or Three Bears Books or something like that to read about. And I had a kid about that, she's not learning anything here. She said, That's not a school book. So, I don't remember, but I think there was some change about that time. And then we went to Parma.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: How did that trip to Parma really get you interested?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Well, we started out with a bay horse and an old grey mule.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: And, well, he took a big wagon with sides up and he built a shelf out on each side.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Your dad is going to ask you about this trip, is he?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Yeah, he's all hopped up to go. He was more of an adventurer. Oh, she thought they do look terrible. The gypsies used to pass through there once in a while. And we'd see them with funny looking outfits and dresses and outfits traveling, horses and things. And she said, Come here, Papa, look out there. Now, there we go.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: See what we look like?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: He said, Oh, now we don't need to look like that. But anyhow, I told her about how to build those things, the wings out this side. Well, anyhow, he put brackets on like a big wide shelf and put those on the side of the wagon near the back end. And it went better than halfway through the middle. And it was on both sides. And that was just wide enough then by those shelves on there to put the bed springs on crossways. The length of the bed spring, we just filled that space in there. And it's all his own idea. He had no picture now. And so they packed everything. And the other thing, well, he had his big tool chest, big big chest about so, you know, about that high and that wide and then that full. About that long. And that was all for his tools and everything. And they had that packed full. And they had one of these old fashioned trunks. I don't know, I believe it was the round top kind. I'm not sure, but the old fashioned trunk. All of our clothes and everything went in there. And everything was packed just right solid. There wasn't a little inch of space to spare. And that was packed in there. And then, what space that didn't take up, we had a tub and a washboard with everything we were going to need when we got there. Well, our dishes and kettles and everything, our dishes were packed in the tub and the washboard, or wash boiler. And so that's an awful good thing to pack them in anyway because it wasn't easily broken. Well, they were all packed in paper and they were all pushed back under that bed. And on top of the bed, there was a, I think you had two feather beds that we always had. Well, those were put on there and then they put all our blankets and sheets and clothes. So our bed was made right in the back end. And then the front half, or not quite half maybe, was taken up with a seat, a driver's seat up there. And then right off, now this is the horses that headed the wagon. And the seat here, they'd sit on facing out so they could see where they're driving. And then right in this corner was a little pot -bellied stove, a little iron stove with a little door that opened on this side. And mid on the top of it. And the pipe went up here out the side of the wagon so we could have a fire if we needed to. It had legs on the bottom. Well, then right next to that stove was, I think it was an orange crate turned on end. And that was stacked full of canned goods and stuff that you would need from place to place. And some of it, well, we didn't have stock everywhere, I mean every stock, everything. But that never got in the short end. They put in a few, add two air kits all the time. And so, and then they had their, our, I'm just choking up. And they had a kettle, no I guess it was a can of water to drink. And then we had the canned fruits and stuff all over there. And then we had, let's see, that's about all I remember except it just had a little bit of, we'd walk around in there between the two places. But you could just sit on the edge of the bed as you rode along. And that made a seat. I'd sit there and ride. But there was a, Edward was riding along. His dad would look up and see a jackrabbit that had to brush. He'd just reach over and grab his gun and let go, bang right over the horse's head. And he never paid any attention to it. And if he saw the thing fall, he'd go out and get it. And maybe he'd partly clean it or draw it. And then bring it on in and Mother would sit there and cut it all up and wash it and dress it all up nice and clean. And put it in her kettle and lay it on. And it was ready for us to eat or else, you know, have it there so it was all clean and ready. And fry it if you wanted to. Or put it on to boil as you drove along. And it's full of hens and sage hens. And the jackrabbit was our chief meat. And well, we'd stop once in a while to pick up something at some little weigh station for a change.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: And maybe Mother would have a kettle of beans on cooking as we rode along.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: And this was where the can was coming from?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Uh huh. And so, that was mostly... Oh, when we got... I don't know. We came to one place where we'd stop. We'd come to little weigh stations like that.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: We'd stop and how far is it to the next town?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: We'd need to know just so we'd know whether we need to stock up with anything that we're out of. Maybe a loaf of fresh bread or something like that. And so, we got to this one place where there was... I don't know what the formation of the rock was there and all. Because I didn't know enough about that. But it was the prettiest rocks I ever saw. And they were just like eggs. And they were from that size clear on up to that big, you know. And just as smooth as silk. And there was a little kind of a creek that had ran there. And that was all full of those snow -white rocks. And sizes and shapes. And we'd pick up the prettiest ones. Edna and I would both walk. We walked, I think, about half the way after we got started. Why, Edna, she could keep up with us, too. She was enjoying it, too. Dad said... Mother said she just knew that Edna would be sick along the way. And 40 miles from a doctor and she'd just have the worst trouble with her. She always was so delicate anyhow. And Dad said, nah, she won't. She'd get started. They said she would probably snap out and be healthier and better. Well, Mother kind of had her doubts, but she was alone with it. She'd open her head to it. And Edna and I walked so much that we'd... You know, the team had to walk. He couldn't hurry them along on the heavy load. So we'd walk along and get on some many pretty rocks. So we'd gather up our dress into a lap and pick them up as we went along. And until it gets so heavy we couldn't walk with that heavy load of rocks in our dress tail there. And finally I just had to dump them all out and start over and run catch up with the wagon. And we just couldn't carry them and couldn't catch up. And I'd hate so bad to give those rocks up, but finally I guess I didn't get away with any of them. I had to dump them all before I got... I had a quick gathering. And we went on to another place and I think it was called Castle Rock. And I don't know whether there's such a place in Idaho or not. And another place, and I want some time to look it up. See if there's a... I don't know, it might be just a nickname. Some of the people gave it to... But I don't know, Castle Rock. And that other place was the Devil's Dive. And that's over a deep canyon and then there was a field built across. And that was a little trouble too. But anyhow, when we got to this Castle Rock, there would be just little rocks, kind of rocks. It would be around here, that big ground you might say.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Coffee canyon or something? Maybe the coffee canyon or something?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Yeah, yes. That big one, maybe a little bigger. A little higher, you know, rounded off. And on top of it was another one, as big as a house. As big as a whole room. And sitting on that little one. And it scared me to death. I just knew that big one was going to roll off onto me. I got close, I guess it had been there a thousand years, but I couldn't see that. I thought that still might fall. But we were driving along and Mother looked over and looking down at the canyon to the one side and said, oh look at that pretty evergreen. And it was a cedar tree. Oh boy, I'd love to have some of that. And Dad said, oh we haven't got time to stop. We better keep on. And she was going to get some branches. But anyhow, I heard what she said. So I remember walking at that time. I guess Edna wasn't. She'd get tired quicker than I would, so she maybe didn't take her nap. You know, time for her nap. But I thought, well I'll run right down there and get her a branch or two of that. She wants some branches of that tree. But I got started and I'd go around one tall rock and get around that one and run across another one this way. I had to weave my way quite a ways down the side hill, way down to the, I guess better than about two blocks long, maybe down that way. And those big old rocks just stacked up all the way down. Big ones sitting on top of the other one.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: So I went down there, and did you ever try to break a cedar bough?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: And I had nothing to break it with, you know. And I twisted and twisted and worked at it. And you just keep on twisting, they will break. And so finally I got some little pieces off the ends that I was able to pull backwards. And then I thought, I've got to get back up there. I'll get lost. They're laden. So I started back up the hill and then I'd run into those awful little, big and little rocks. Big and some of the little ones. And I had to go way around them. They'd go clear and get in the way of another one to get there. Finally I got back up to the road with my little, a few little twigs. And got up there and they're in the road and ran on down. And I couldn't see the wagon. And I kept running and running. And I couldn't see the wagon anywhere. And finally I could see where the road branched. And that's quite a ways down there. And I owned the land. I hurried as fast as I could down there to see, to get to that so I could see which way the wagon went. See if I could get the wagon. And there was no wagon in sight on either road. So I guess that's, I guess something told me that it was time to pray. And I was lost. And I thought, I just said, oh Lord, take me to the right road. I hope I don't get on the wrong one because I'll get that much further behind. And I just stopped there and thought about it. And I wasn't very old, but I, my grandmother and all those people were very religious. And I knew nothing but when you were in trouble that's where to go for help. So I prayed there that the Lord didn't need you. And now I thought, I'll take this road and if there are ways down there, and then if I can't see around the bend, I'll come back and go to the other one. And I was in the study and finally I took the one road and that's when I got me to come back. But when I got on that road, I got told that finally I could see way in the distance I could see the wagon. Well, I run until I'd get out of breath and they hadn't missed me. No, they didn't know what I was, I was right along behind the wagon. I was, and when I got there I was puffing and crying.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: And I said, why don't you wait for me? And they said, well, for you?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: I said, well, you know, I didn't think they thought you were right behind the wagon. And I gave her this little bunch of twigs.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: And they felt bad at the time, they couldn't hardly help but laugh either because I was horrified, I was so scared. But I just stood there and prayed to myself, but I don't know any idea. And well, I had examined the tracks too, I was examining the tracks and they looked just as fresh on one road as they did the other. I couldn't tell by that. That's when I asked for help. I got it. So that was one adventure, and then we went on down. At some time, I don't know which was first or which was last, but I remember these incidents stand out. And we went across this deep canyon in the field and it had been raining. And it was just mud, hub deep.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: And we had gotten out into the middle of this field and got stuck. And there was no way of getting out, we tried and tried. And pretty soon we saw a rig coming. And he came down and it was mud just so deep and the field was quite narrow. And there was no way of him passing us. And he was just a young fellow, quite a young man. I guess it was on Sunday. And I guess he was going to church or going to see his sweetie or going to get married or something because he was dressed up as a slicker and a whistle. And he just dressed in shoes, shining. You could see yourself in them. It looked like a livery team, you know, a driving team, a pretty driving team, and buggy. And he couldn't get anywhere and we couldn't get anywhere. So finally they had to unhitch his team. And he took one horse and grabbed the other one. And of course they were both waiting in mud with a nice pressed suit on and his shoes shining. And they got down and they brought each horse down and then hitched them on to our team. And they got the four other horses together. And then they managed to... They were pushing and finally got us out of there. And then they took us on to the end of the rope and then hitched his team back up and let him go back through there again and he could go on through. It was like bugging. So, I'm going to add, they called out the devil to die and said, I think the devil's going to get us before we got out of there.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: I never did know that man's name or where he was going, but I felt so awful sorry for him. I was awful glad to help us out. And then we got about across to the... Well, while we were on the desert first, we went to the... We stopped at Little Way Station there, the store, and inquired the man how far he was until the next town. We just wondered if they'd get any... you know, if you were carrying any load in your friend, you had to. They had quite a load as it was. And we stocked up just at the last thing. And they told him, well, that was quite a ways. They said it's about a two days drive.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Dad thought, what?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: They said, yes, this is the edge of the desert. And we didn't know we had a desert to cross and weren't prepared for it.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: So, he said, are you prepared to have kept water for it?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: No. Dad said, no, they didn't have it. And so, the only thing to do, I think we spent the night there. We kept near this station because Dad asked if he had a barrel he would sell us for water. No, he says, I haven't. Everybody that's been going along here has been the same way. And they said, I'm all out of the barrels. He said, come to think about it, I think I've got a little ten gallon keg out there that might help. Better nothing. And so, they filled up with water for overnight. See, they couldn't tighten it together. And meanwhile, Mother and Dad got the boiler and the tub out full of dishes and things. And unpacked all those and got wooden boxes from the fellas, packed the dishes in. And then they filled the tub and the boiler with water. And of course, that slush drove it all. And then they filled all they could for us for drinking water. They filled up everything that was empty we could fill. And so, we made up our minds that we wouldn't drink any water if we could help it. We'd just do it out and if we got too thirsty, we'd open a can of cherries or something, you know, something that had juice in it and make out with that. And so, well, then they decided, we started out early. And the next morning, they took out that fix and Dad built a little shelf on the back end of the wagon and set that little barrel on. And it got early and started off over across the desert. And Mother and Dad slept out that night. They did, once in a while, they'd leave and let us kids have the bed, you know, to ourselves. And then they'd sleep on the ground and it was nice and fresh air, you know. So, they were sleeping out that night and they said they listened and they'd always hobble on the horses. And they couldn't hear his old chains jingling and jangling.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: So, they'd listen for him quite a bit and they couldn't hear a thing and that was unusual that they could be that quiet. Even if they were resting.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: And so, as soon as it began to break daylight, so you could see the forms now, Dad was up and gone. And they said he had to go look after those. He took it and they'd taken it out for, they didn't care for this daddy. They probably took the road for home. And he was gone quite a while and he found the tracks where they had started home, or started back. And he told Mother, he said, well, they've gone, they've taken the back track. And he said, I'll have to go after them. And said, you'll just have to hold the forte now until I get back and do the best you can and I'll be back as quick as I can. And it was just about noon when he got back. And he'd run half the way, just basically. And then he got on the horse and rode one of them and led the other one. So, that's the time we got left in the desert. Out in the desert and no water. And then when he got back there, he hitched him up and started driving on. And when eating came, he started to water the horses so there wasn't a drop of water. That barrel had leaked, it didn't slow enough and nothing else. So, he had to get the water out of the tub and boiler. And they were naturally thirsty.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: And so, then, I don't know, I guess he went as far as he could that night. And anyhow, he started dawning and got to the edge of the, I don't know which was first and which was last anymore. But he got over to the edge of the desert and they came to, there was a creek there. And you know those horses wouldn't know where to go across that bridge and there was a fly. They went right down through the creek. They were thirsty and they were hunting water. They said, go ahead and have a good drink. But they had to hook him up and they said he didn't dare go out and have too often much of it after that long. And they said, gosh, if I smell it, he'd have some sick horses on his hands. So, they went and had a good drink and it went on. And we set camp that night. It was getting long toward, I think, late in the afternoon or something. And we went up to, there was a place where there was a man was threshing and a farmer there. They had threshing crew at their place at that time. And Dad asked the man if he cared if we camped near there or something. I'm afraid he bought our hand in there. He said that was all right. He said, no, don't camp out there. He said, drive in a lot here and camp there. And they wanted us to make ourselves at home and just make us comfortable. And he said, well, we drive in, you know, just drive away from in there. Make ourselves at home that way. And they turned their horses, howled the horses and turned them away. Well, we got ready to, well, when they got up, you know, got breakfast over by. And we went to take a look and get the horses going. And the old mule was laying there, foundered, puffed up like a toy balloon. And he was just down and couldn't get up.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: And we thought, now what'll he do on top of everything else?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: And so, they then helped him and they kind of held us. And I'll court over him, I guess, to decide what to do about him. And they hadn't thought he was going to die right there. They were going to have to kill him or something. And the old woman fell asleep. The only thing I think you can do that may help him, he doesn't know what that says, but he had seen it done. And they decided to stick him. And so they took a knife and stuck him just near the rib, you know, the head of the hip bone there somewhere. And I remember they stuck him to release his gas. He was well set, his hoof's big. And they left him for a while. And between the men, all kinds of holtas and helped him out. They had to put him up on his feet because he couldn't get up.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Just hadn't had it before he left him up? Huh?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Just hadn't left him up. Yeah. And the whole crew, the thrashing crew, they got after him and got him up on his feet. And then after he got him up there, he could stand, but he couldn't. Well, they had kind of, I don't know, they left him a little while or got him to walk a little bit. But anyhow, I said, now the only thing you can do is, and you're taking a long chance, but it's a chance. The only thing you can do is just put him in his harness and get to walking. And we had to walk it off of him and keep him going. And they had to put him in the harness and put him on. He didn't look like a very promising prospect, but his poor old fellow, he felt sorry for him. And him kind of poking along, so he kind of dove up and stiffed. He went down to, he was, he was all right the night. Anyhow, he'd gotten, he made the rest of the trip, anyhow. And we came in then to Parma.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Parma, they, well, we...

speaker: Rob Moore
words: How long did it take to have a daily routine on this trip?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: You know, you mean the eats and the... Well, I mean, just getting to know what time you were going to have the meals and stuff like that. Oh, well, we got started pretty early. It's because we didn't care about being any longer, especially on that desert, isn't necessary. And we'd get up just about regular, as soon as it's good daylight, and get to go in for breakfast and everything. We'd eat this for a good long. We had all our cooked stuff, and if we didn't, we cooked it along the road, had dinner there, had it ready to eat, time we got, probably wanted to eat it.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: And they drove it to us.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: And your dad would just get up early in the morning and the horses all hitched up, and all your stuff would be ready?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Well, no, he'd just get them up and get them watered, and they'd already graze at night. So it had been taken by humans to get them harnessed and all that. We'd just eat while Mother would fix the lunch, while we were... while he was getting the harness on the horses, maybe, or something. And they'd carry the bottle there, just wash our hands, and go ahead and eat. My father had already cooked, or maybe she'd cook right on the... she had her skillet and kettles right in the... and then sometimes we'd buy something that was already cooked, you know. And we didn't have a little trouble there, we ate regular meals at regular times. Lunch's a bit between if we wanted to.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Would you travel until dark, or stop at a likely spot?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Well, we just traveled about as long as we... unless the horses were unusually tired or something, they might stop a little early, but we traveled just about dark, just before dark, you know, dusk. And so she'd get the horses unhitched and hobbled, and then we could have her supper while they graze around.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: How long would you have to eat?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: I don't remember. It was... Well, I don't know whether it was about a week or... it was less than a week or what. I don't even remember.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Anything less than a week?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: I think it was, because I never even thought of it much to ask those questions while Dad was still living, or Mother either. Now that they're gone, I wish I knew some of those things. And I was just longing for the trip, I didn't keep any...

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Did you enjoy the trip for a minute?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Oh, wonderful. I wish I could go over again to see where it was.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Would you want to go by horse and wagon or anything?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Yes, I would. I just didn't have anything urgent to keep me from it and be kind of fun.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: So then you settled in in Parma.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Mm -hmm. No, we drove in Parma. We drove up to... Well, in Parma, the streets run the railroad run this way, and the town was laid out pretty much in the same angle there. Well, along here, along Main Street, there was a saloon down here, and that's where Dad stopped and went in there to ask directions or something about work or something. And that man's name was Lang. And he talked to him a while, and he came back out, and he had... There's a move there that I... I don't know that it'll be moved once in the rest, or in Parma. But I can't remember how it came or just how long they had it. I think in the first place, they lived across the track on this side, and the mother... Oh, I know there was a lady came through teaching flower making and all the fancy flowers and everything, and we had gotten acquainted with Mrs. Lang through the men folks, and Mrs. Lang wanted to take that flower course, but she didn't want to take it alone. She was kind of timid. And she told Mother, she said, I'll pay for the course and I said, if you'll take it with me, if you'll go with me. So she took the course in flower making there, and that's the only thing I remember. Well, no, it was kind of a hotel, a rooming house, I guess it was, or hotel, something there, not far from where we lived on that side of the track. And it was... I just remember it by the... They had a big family, and one of the older boys was married, and because of their names. Their name was Cunningham, firstly. But it was Molly, Flora, Cora, Laura, Nora, and Dora. Molly sounds kind of out of place. She was the daughter -in -law, I think it was. I believe it was Molly, Flora, Cora, Laura, Nora, Dora. And that kind of kept a sing -song to it with me, and I remembered it. And I was always running around saying to myself, when I was a kid, I said, in the whole spiel, I'm on the floor for her. Dora and the other. But I don't think that lasted long. I don't know how they come to move, because they moved over across the track, and Dad bought this little place, right next door to Lang's. And Lang's had a nice big house. There was a little place there that he bought for sale, and they moved over there, and that's where we were living when we got that picture.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: And Albert was a boy. They just had one youngster, and he was, I guess, about my age. Might have been just a little older than I. Albert Lang.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: I was going to say, you came over to Troy very soon after that. Yeah, I was promoted to the fourth or fifth grade. It wasn't quite time for school to be out. When we left there, we were going to come on over to Troy. But the teacher promoted me anyway. I said, no, Osbridge is going to have to go backwards. I said, no, it's fine. I said, you're ready for it.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Are you ready for the next grade?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: I was a fine teacher. And we started then.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Another thing I skipped was from Laramie to where? The mushroom?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Yes. We were on the train, and we had to go around by Pendleton, somewhere or other. We were out somewhere. We went around and over into Pendleton, then out again. And that's the first Indians I ever saw. And the railroad depot was, there were just a lot of Indians in the depot, and some of them ran around with their blankets, you know, holding their blankets around them, and their feathers and things. And I was scared to death of them because I'd heard them talking about war so much, you know, the Indian wars. I'd heard all my granddad and uncles and some of them.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: They had still talked about it when you were growing up?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Yeah, they were still talking about, telling me about something, about the clashes, you know, about the civil war and all that. I'd heard so much about war that I just thought those Indians would eat us up, I guess. So, and the mother and dad started to go. He left me, but that's, I guess, where we met, that must have been where we, we were with the Lees when we went to Rexburg, because I think they were with us on that trip. We must have got ahold of them in Laramie.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: But, anyhow, they put Edna, Edna didn't feel very well, and they put her to sleep on the benches, on the benches, and put her coats down and covered her up, and let her rest because she was worn out. And mother and dad went down to town where that was how far they had to go now, and they, we had to wait for her to change cars in Pendleton, and they had to wait there, I don't know how long, and they got to get something to eat, and they got some sausage and bread and stuff and come back with it, something we could lunch on. There was no other way of getting anything like that. It was going to be a long time before we got to where we were going, so that's when they went down. When they came back, Edna was wiggling and fretting, and mother went over to look at her, and she was literally covered with bedbugs. That depot was just alive with bedbugs. And you just look after it, you know, you've got them stirred up, and that is after dark they'll come out, and our clothes that she did spread over them, her were all full of them, and the other about had to fit, and that was our experience in Pendleton. But anyhow, skipping that place, we got, after we were coming to Troy, why, we came by train to Troy.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Why did the folks decide to move to Troy?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Well, we were headed that way then because of her relatives. Mother's relatives were there. Her aunt and uncle and their family and then cousins. They were all in the sawmill business. It's down here at the Dinsmore Mill, you know, down here two miles and a half this side of Troy, between the highway and the, oh, what's that, Spring Valley. It is just, well, where that barn is, you know, on the highway. And the flat and the bridge, you know. Well, that's, that is our playground for our school, and up the raise, just a little way up the hill, from that was the schoolhouse, moved away from there. And we lived just a little further down, and down there where Harry Glazer lives now. That is my aunt and uncle's place.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: And they had a mill there?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: And there's a big sawmill there down on the, next to the creek, just above there. And, oh, I have pictures somewhere of it, but I don't know.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Was this move to Troy more your mother's idea, do you think, than the other one?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Well, I think they had, had had word from them, or something, that they, was the possibility of, Dad was a sawmill man, too. And he was in the East. He was obviously running a sawmill, he was an engineer, he was a sawyer, he was anything that they wanted. He was a sawmill man. So he was coming out, and this original idea that, that, uh, Uncle had, Mr. Dinsmore.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Did you know Homer Dinsmore?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: No, I guess you're too young to know him. Because he had that first, uh, that, that, uh, garage there, Philly station. And that's the first one that was there, you know, that was the first, what was it, first gasoline, or first, so some kind of name had it. And anyhow, that was the first Philly station they had in, at that time. He, he was a one -legged man, he'd had, he'd lost his leg when he was just 21 years old. And, but anyhow.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: His name was Ann? Huh?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: His name was... His father's name, Homer, was the one that had that station. Homer Dinsmore. And, uh, Addison was his name, and Uncle Ed, and, uh, Liddy. But they, them, and cousin Connelly Smith, he used to be in Moscow there. And then mother, or dad, and his dad, my grandfather, they, uh, was kind of, they thought they'd go into the mill business. And he had a pretty good crew amongst themselves. Well, it happened, and now in the long run, that they kind of got, changed things around until Uncle Ed was kind of the boss of it, and the rest of them kind of did the other work. So, that was,

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: that was, that's where my baby brother was born. 1904, no, yeah, 1904.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: I went to school there three years.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: John Ogden was the teacher. He was a Troy from Troy. And, he, incidentally, later on, taught out here at Helmer. And, and his son lived there in Moscow until he died a couple, three years ago, something like that. But his family still lives right there in town. Right near the center of town, just as you come out.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Are you in the Queen's of Moscow much?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Well, they live out on the, well, I'm not going to tell you, I guess, but, you know, you come to Troy Road, out to the, the, uh, doghouse.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: What is that?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: The, uh, for the doctors, doctors, zimmer, zimmer. Yeah, I said the only thing I think of is the doghouse. They had taken a doghouse. Well, they just live a block north of him and a couple blocks, two blocks up that way, and then a couple, two blocks back toward town. They just lived, that's where his family is. And they were, I've known them ever since, I've only just lived with the kids. Well, I, I knew their dads since he had his first little short britches on.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Was that area a pre -driving area when you lived there?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Well, yes, quite so. There was a, oh, there was other families lived up there. There was the Sawyers and the Vandiventors and the, I don't know, several families. The Nesbitts and the, oh, the, the, up on the hill, the Martinsons were right there close.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Did your folks buy you, buy land or rent or what?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: No, they just had that, that little house. It was just part of the, I don't know who had that mill before they got it.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: But I guess they must, that house must have went with the rest of the business.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: And I think that probably the folks had written them, I don't know, maybe when they were back east, that they might have heard from them that they were out there in the mill business and they thought there was a good chance for them to get a job there, get work, you know. They might have, somewhere along the line they heard from them.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: And then you went to school for a couple of years then?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Three years. And then they moved to the homestead up here.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Why did they decide to move out to the homestead?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Well, they kind of thought it would be kind of nice to have 160 acres of their own land.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: And so they just thought they'd take it, they thought that might sound like a good idea, that it was free if you just had to work on it, you know, had to live there so much time out of the year. I know we went up there, you had to live there each year anyhow, a little while. And we went up one time, I don't know, in the winter time, I don't know what time exactly, except it was winter, snowy time. And we went up to, I don't know, stay a week or something like that, so stuff, pull our rights. And we got to the, you know, this long road, you go here down from Helmer, you go to this round house there at the most known...

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: I can't think of it, I guess it doesn't matter. But just before you cross the track, you know, coming this way, that big brown house, well, you turn in front of that and go turn to your right and go off out that way, and then you cross the railroad that you get so far down, and all the way down to the forks of Potlatch, and then you swing over to the McGarry, used to be a McGarry place, as a kind of lookout upon the knoll right alongside the road, but it was just cut off the bank. And then you turn and swing right back, then you start waving on up to the Miller place, and that was, oh, I don't know, a few miles. And that's where we were going to stay. Well, the Felts had a place, Ed Felts, and a homestead there. And his son, or his uncle and wife lived there with him, and Millers were just right, just a fence between them, just to know, as far as we hear, the close neighbors here, you know, and lived in this fence. But we got there and the snow wasn't plowed, would you know, or it wasn't plowed out or anything. And we stopped there to talk to this woman, and I think, I don't know who it was that took us out, but anyhow, we got there and the sled tipped us away and just jumped us on out there in the snow. My brother was just a baby.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Were you around looking for the place you were?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Well, no, we stopped and inquired something other about it. I don't know whether we had already made arrangements with Mr. Miller to stay at his place until we could get a place fixed, you know, and make improvements. And anyhow, I know that the sled upset him. Well, we took all of his supplies, you know, with us to last, while we were going to be up there. There was matches all over the top of the snow, or eats and things that were there. But I remember thinking that those matches, I guess a whole box of matches, were strewn out all over the top of the snow.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: When they moved out from Troy to the homestead, what kind of rig did you use?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Well, that time was just a short trip that I had to go into, oh to kill time, I guess improvement time or something like that. And then we went back out, but then when we decided to live there, we had lived there, let's see, the spring of 1905 or something, let's see, I don't know, the spring, after school was out, we went there the last of May, I think it was, the first of May, sometime in May, that would live there all that summer, and all that winter, and then the next spring would be the one whole year.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: I don't know just how the time works out, but anyhow, it's in October, must have been October the next year, because we missed, yes, we missed that whole term of school, and then going on through the time until the next October, we started school, Sister and I. And that was in 1907,

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: and then October 31st, Halloween.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Back to the homestead, your whole family moved out there in the spring of the year?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Yeah, we all moved out there and started to work on it, we plowed out our road and graded. Where did you stay when you first... Well, Miller's, you see, this road came through this way, and this fence was here, and Peltz lived here, and Miller's lived here, and Miller had a little sawmill on his own out there, but he had a little house up on the hill, this road to here, and up on the hill from his place, it wasn't, just a raise, it wasn't any hill to Mount Denny, that's where they moved to, when they first went out to batch, until they got their house built, and so they let us use that, how we built ours, and we just moved into their little place there.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Were the Millers and the Peltz the only people around here?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Well, there's the two Peltzes and the Millers and the Ora Hays and the Hollenbegs, and, oh, there was quite a group back in there, they weren't real close together, some of them were quite close, but the older Peltz, Hampton Peltz, their place was on up, they was on north, or south of us, and they didn't have any place to go then either, they'd go back and forth to this son's place, this nephew, and they finally decided to put up a cabin, up on their place, well they had a big house raising there that day, and all the neighbours gathered in with their lunch and the men that they saw as the next, and everything, and they, we went to work that day and they put up a cabin for them, and they had lunch, yes, that's the main parts of it, and the men all turned up, as well as the Hollenbegs and the Gherys and the Hays, and, oh, there's three bachelors, I can't think of their names, and Millers and the two Peltz, and us,

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: that's all I think I just know, and the house raising, and Mrs.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Peltz, she was one of the neighbours, she was walking nice to us and all, and she said, after we moved out there, we'd go to her place, she said send over, many times, she just had all kinds of milk to throw away, and the camera wouldn't skim milk for anything too, and she was making cheese out of it too, so Edna was sent, Mother sent Edna over one day, just a little vase over there, to get a pail of milk, and while she was over there, this little kid was sitting in his high chair, and he was a messy little thing, you know, he always had his hands in there, and he had a thing smeared all over, and so she wanted Edna to eat supper with him, and Edna wasn't hungry, she said, Mother, but she said, do you like smear case? And Edna didn't know what to say, because she didn't know, she thought, she said she didn't know what smear case was, and she thought she must have been the little kid that smeared, so Edna just smeared with everything all over her clothes, and she said she didn't know how to answer, I don't know what answer she gave, but she didn't know what, because she never heard of smear case, she didn't know what it was, What was smear case?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Well, that's, had its cheese, that's the German name for smear case.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: From your place, or from Miller's place to your place?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Oh, to our home, said Kevin, from there it was about a mile.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Was there a trail there?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Oh, we made a trail, we started in, there was a, oh, maybe a quarter of a mile, or not quite that much, from Miller's, because I went up a slope for about a quarter of a mile, and then we branched off to the right, to go to our place, and this went on up to the Hayes homestead, and all those, but we branched off to the right and made our riding road, and from there on, or dad and I, and mother and I too sometimes,

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: sometimes we'd all go, but we took the youngster, he was a little tyke, and fixed him a little cart, I think a two wheeled cart, and take him in that and draw him.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: And how Edna would be doing it, because she's supposed to take care of the baby, and sitting right there where we were, you know, but she had one day, and the wheel wore some kind of a stick, these branches, you know, these things, and she had a little hatchet, and she's chopping these off, and she chopped her finger.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Edna sounds like she got in a lot of trouble with that. She was taking care of the baby, but I guess she didn't know that she chopped pretty badly.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: How would you make the road?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Oh, we had to cut brush and fall trees, and grade it.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: How much of that could you help your dad with?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Oh, well, my mother helped him, and there would come a day when she'd have to stay home and bake up some bread, and cook up some things ahead of time, and maybe a good kettle of beans, and something that she could keep over and have to fall back on. So I'd go with him that day. So we'd roll the logs, and you know, with a cry pole, we'd use that as a TV. And a big flip saw, or a big, that's a six foot saw, and then two man saw.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: You'd be on one end, and you'd end on the other end?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Yeah, we'd build a bridge, and we had to extend those corduroy bridges, put the poles and things on the stringers, across a little ditch, a little creek we had there. And then, that's when I got my foot smashed.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: It was a neighbor, a friend from out of town, had gotten, had come in there to, I think, to Miller's for a visit or something, and they had to ask him if he could hire his horses for the day to call. And we were going around the hillside, you know, cutting, after we crossed the bridge, probably somewhere on this side of the bridge too, but we had a fiction to plow that up there so we could level it down, you know, and make our road, on the slant. And he said yes, so we could take it on, and make it use, and we had those great big logging horses. So, they had to go under the horses, and the bridge wasn't finished yet. We had the stringers in, and the whole part of the floor laid, that is the corduroy lay, but anyhow, we had to go down through this little creek, just marshy and all mud, and then go up the other side. And so he took one of them on up that way, and up on the other side, and he told me to, what he meant, I don't know just how he said, but I misunderstood him. And he said, for me to hold that one, and I thought he wanted me to follow, you know, and go along, so I held it until he got out of the way, and then I started with my horse, and I had him close under the bits, you know, under the reins. So, my kids saw him, and I didn't hardly come to his back, and he was a big elephant, and when he got down to it, oh the creek wasn't, oh it wasn't any wider, just marshy, and soft from here to his chair there, I guess. But it was a little bit of water running there, placed to him, but it was all, you know, when the horse stepped that heavy, old foot of his, but he'd go down deep through that marshy place there. And so I started, and I hadn't held him up before, I thought he was in good control, but he just slinged me down like a lip crack.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: I'd hold him, and then I started to go through with him, and leading him, and then he started plunging and munching, you know, just doing a hurry. And that's when I began swinging around, and putting her off my feet, and swinging this way, I got the feet crossed somewhere, I had to swing back, and in the long run, I had him on this way, and he stepped right down this way. I was on this big old, just big old hoofs, very big thing, and if it hadn't been that that was marshy ground, just wet and sandy, and mud, I didn't crush him onto my foot, I guess. But I think it weakened, it stretched some of the ligaments and all, but I don't think it broke anything, but it just buried that thing in soft mud, the whole way through the horse.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: And I said, this hurts the bad, I was about to cry, and he said, why in the world did you start that big horse through there?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: And I said, you told me to, I said, I told you to hold him, boy, I tied up that other one, and come back and get him.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: I was just supposed to hold him until he came back after him. Just the time you were in on sympathy, he wasn't even too understanding.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: No, but he couldn't see it, he thought that I had, he didn't know what I had, that I had understood his instructions.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: But anyhow, I had a pretty sore foot for a week or two.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: I don't have something.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: How long did it take you to fix up that road?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Oh, I don't know, we worked, we worked on it a good portion of the summer, and I guess, we went out there in the spring, and we worked a month or two, I guess. I don't know how long it took us, but anyhow, we got that road fixed in over two, it was about three quarters of a mile of it, anyhow, it was our own individual road, and there was that other quarter of a mile that was already made to the other man's homestead, and anyhow, we had to get our cabin built then, and we didn't have a log cabin, we had a road finished then, so we took in a load of lumber, I guess we took it from the, got it from the mill there, the folks, you see, would get it right from around the mill. We got in a load of lumber, then we helped build a house. Dad was doing most of the building, but I was always, my mother and father was always there to hand me this and bring me that, and reach up such and such a kind of a stick, and, I don't know, so.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Why wouldn't, why wouldn't, these neighbors of yours had a house raising, why couldn't you, you had a house raising too? Well, they didn't think of it, that's after we'd been out, just before we left the homestead, that they did that for the Feltz's, and there'd been more of us out there by that time, and we got this all neighboring more, and When you were first out there, were you pretty much on your own?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Well, there was a few that took time kind of to get acquainted, and they were all good neighbors and everything, an awful fine bunch, and then at that time I met Durney Palmer on the homestead, and he was working out here toward Neva, and he had all those mines out there then, and you know there's an old stamp mill, or an old, some kind of a mining contraption out there, but that's where his mine was, he and his nephew and another uncle that they had, they were working that mine, and then they had to go out every so often and work out their dues or whatever it is they called it, so.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Well, he was, I was going to say that on Saturday nights after you all got a queen out there, well that's, we had met the Hollandaics, were living out in that part, and they came out west first, and they had their homes dead, but it was beyond ours, on the further west, east, and we were south But anyhow, the Hollandaics had known the dismissed in Missouri, and the Hollandaics came out, well they were at our house most of the time, Mrs. Hollanbeck and the kids, and they lived there with us for a while, and I guess then they got things fixed up so they could live on their own place, and so anyhow, in the long run, we got to be quite a bunch And then the Smiths came out west, and Ernie and Henry and I don't know if there's a lot of them, the relatives of theirs, that went to work up at that mine, I mean the Goldenrod mine there were then, that's just the site of all that other machinery in the place And they knew Hollanbeck there, and so Hollanbeck brought them over, the folks told them to bring them on over, and get a queen with us, and they brought them over on Saturday when they didn't have to work, they'd bring them over there and they'd stay overnight, and go back Sunday, it was ever so many miles, twelve miles or something, they had to come over there, but they'd been used to thatching And the home cooking tasted pretty good, I don't know, but the mother was a good cook, so we had the garden in, and we used to have the wilted lettuce, and we had our own chickens, and we had fried chicken, and well, we'd have homemade bread and all the things like that that they weren't used to And then we'd have all the buttermilk and butter and sweet milk that they could hold, and they'd come over every Saturday and spend the weekend And so, there's the hails in the same bunch, Mr. and Mrs.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Hale, they were kind of elderly people, and maybe a herd of grandma Hale that died over here at the rest home, the county home, at the age of a hundred And she was just a little bit out there, but I don't know, it'd be a whole bunch of them, they just had a wait on the time It was kind of fun, all kinds of deliciousness, and everybody would sit around and talk about something funny that had happened, and visit Did you enjoy those time at the homestead?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Yeah, yes, yes I did, and my own mother was kind of particular with us kids about, we didn't just tear around like tomboys and rangatangs, you know, she expected us to act a half -way lady -like So while we were there and going to school and all, we were just kind of calm, but we got on the homestead and we just turned loose, kind of And we spent a little long time with a saw or an axe or something like that and just roughing it, you know And mother was riding a bunch, and we cut to about a little tiny jack pines, I guess they called them, that we were all through there We just cut out a whole mess of those and peeled them, snow white, and then built a fence all around our yard We fixed them all up pretty, like that, we didn't have anything else to do, and that was something to kill time We were just out there by ourselves, so mother and, well most of the time, Edna had to take care of the baby, she didn't have so much else to do But mother and I built a fence and cut out the trees, and we had a log house

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: And the particular kind of, usually lots of people have these bunch of little square buildings out in the backyard with a little window in the side Of course it was four posts, and well, they had fixed a fine seat on it now, but four posts, but it was all the, what is it, air conditioned It had shakes all over the top of the roof, and then there was gunny sides, and it was just breezy, it was a good thing There was nobody there with family anyway We had the door, well they had their door and everything, it was all just as fine as you please But we had a chicken house, a wooden, I mean a log, a little log chicken house, and we had a barn, and we had a cow out there then And we ran out of flour and bread one time for quite a while, but we hadn't had any bread, and it would be quite a while for the next supply to come in You'd make a fairly list of everything that you had to have, and oh about maybe once a week or every two weeks or whatever it was But somebody would go out with
