the wagon and bring in a load of supplies, and each fellow had turned in his slip of what he needed And they'd keep track of that and they'd bring it in and they'd do whatever they got back, because if they'd have reached when they'd come back, why they'd go, each one go and get his load and come in for them And we ran out of bread one time, and we didn't have any flour, so my grandmother decided, well, we want some bread She went down to the barn and got some shorts that they would be fed to cow, and it was nice clean shorts, and she mixed up bread And we thought that was kind of funny, same as the old cow did, but we ate that and mine was the best stuff I ever did Well, that's wheat, kind of, it's a certain grind, and it's kind of like whole wheat, it's all ground up, you know, kind of like whole wheat, it's brand and all right in it And it was sure the best bread, we were kind of sorry then when we got the good bread, the good wheat back here, the good flour, it was awfully good Did you eat quite a bit of wild game and stuff?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: No, we didn't, not at that time, we didn't, I don't think we ever had any while we were there I thought you said you were chewing something Well, that was wild game too, but I was thinking of, you meant the deer and elf and things, but we didn't get any deer and elf I think that's why I'd have the, it'd be the pheasants and the squirrels and things like that, because that's about all we did, we had plenty of pheasants and squirrels And those little pine squirrels, you know, some people don't eat them, but they were very good

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Did you get to do a pretty good shot?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Yes, I did, that was a good shot, I guess, after the boy, the neighbor boy, he was from the brooks as they lived over west of us And they had to come up this way to the very road, then come on over to our place And, oh I think it was only about quarter of a mile square across, but you had to go down through a canyon over his place And he was going to go out during the summer and take in the harvest, and he brought his gun over him and said I'd keep it dig up back here And he said, here's some shells, you went there, I want you to use them now And I said, I don't like it, I'm afraid of yours, I'm not going to ruin it, and he said you can't hurt that He knew I'd been shooting ours, it was just a little single shot, .22, and he knew I'd been shooting ours all the time

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: I told him like that, he said no you can't hurt it, and I want you to use it He said keep it firm and give back, because that little one needed in the house over there, so it may not be that when I got back So I kept it in, I got so I could take them off, so quite often I'd get picked off feathers on the fly Who was 22?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Uh huh, that's 22

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: I'd go over after mail, let me go over after mail over to Miller's and get the mail Whoever was out would bring in mail and you could go down and get it And I'd go after, and then come back and make a feather to two or a squirrel or something

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Did your, did your mother like this time in the woods?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Yes I think she did, I really think she liked it And we would, if it hadn't been for school, we'd probably stayed there a long while But they said we better get these dumb bunnies into school, they don't expect them to learn anything

speaker: Rob Moore
words: You lived a pretty different life, the most interesting thing you could do Yes, yes we just turned loose and we did everything on, well roughing it just as a man's style We did the man's work, Roy's work around, we made us a garden, a nice big garden, we had chickens And one old rooster, big old, I think that one was a rock island, no, a red Roll down?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Roll down, roll down red, big old thing, and he was the fastest old fella you ever saw And he'd get in and call all the lady friends in the garden with him and scratch around in there And we'd have to watch him, we didn't have a garden fenced off separate, and we'd have to watch to keep him out of it And then we'd go chase him out, and Dad would go out and chase him out in a shoe, and when he did the shoe very good He just kind of balked and strutted and he didn't go very far, and Dad would pick up a cloth and throw at him And that would make him pretty mad, and he was resentful of Dad wanting him to be out of there And then finally when Dad would think he got him far enough, he'd turn to go back in the house and the fella would jump up on anything he could find A poster, stump or anything, and crow just lounging good, thinking like that, so that's as much as say he'd run the battle

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: And we had another Klimtterrauk rooster, we raised him from a, we hatched him from a egg He was the gangliest, most un -gangly looking thing you ever saw, and he was growing up with the rest of them And so anyone got a crown of anything that he wanted, he'd pack them, chase them away, and so I'd kick at him and make him stay out of the way And let the others have something to eat, and he'd come so he'd fly at my foot when I'd kick at him And he would, oh he got to be half grown or something, and that got to be a game between us And so I'd kick at him and get him to fly at me, and every time Mother would go outside, well the house sat here, and around the back of the house It was around, from the back of the house, around to the front, there was a tree near the end of the house And we had a meat cage that had it there, we had plenty of sacks all around, and we had those sacks in the shade of the tree And we'd just keep meat, just as nice as a nice box And down at the edge of the house, we had a buried box, a good sized box with a lid on, and we kept the butter and everything like that, you know, to keep it nice and cold And she'd have it with claws over the butter So,

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: after Mother would start out here, we had a box nailed up on the back of the house back here too, that she'd keep milk in And she had a screen over the front of it, and she'd go around the house and all, and she'd get a pan of milk for the meal And she'd go around there, and pretty soon I'd hear her yell, and the little rooster would fly up at her, she would scare the death out of him And he'd fly at her when she'd go around, and every time she'd go around the house

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: And when we'd come in, after we moved in time, we brought him in here, we brought our chickens in, and the lid right over here was in this next corner And he would fight with Hayes' old rooster, and he'd go down to the other corner of the same block And she came running over, Mrs. Hayes came running over one day and said, oh, Mr. Widdon, come over and chop his head off or something like that And she'd cut her chicken's head off, and said, you're old rooster, mind about her fighting, and he'd about killed him or something like that Had to put him out of his misery, and she was really cooking, I think And they'd fight, and then my aunt and uncle came up from business one time from Troy, and they brought me a pair of panties up here And that's for Elman, for my brother, he thought those were pretty nice Well, this old big Plymouth Rock rooster, and that little bandy would fight all the time And that big old Plymouth Rock flicked everything he came in and fed up with until he caught that baddie And the baddie could just, he'd get ready to jump in the bandy, go underneath it, hit him from the back end, and just jump between his legs He was so quick and so little, and in the time he would get turned around, he was back someplace else

speaker: Rob Moore
words: What other things could you do when your dad was building the house with you? What other things could you help him with? Well, when we were building the house, we made our own shakes and shingles, and made shakes for the barn, it was a big barn too Would you do that?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: I helped, oh I could slip the shakes on a shingle, and he would shave them, and they made the hand shaved, you know, they're tapered and just like the regular factory shingles And I could slip shakes, alright, with the fro, the rallett

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: And that's, oh I don't know, I could, and then besides I knew the name of all the tools he wanted, you know, and I could go get whatever he wanted and help out with it Would you have rather stayed out on the homestead, or were you glad to come back into town?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Well, I was glad to come back to school, I knew I had to go to school, I wanted to do that, but besides Matt, I just found out the homestead, I was enjoying it But I couldn't get any education out there, so it was a case of moving in

speaker: Rob Moore
words: And when was it that you did move into Bovill? When was it that you did move into Bovill?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Oh that we did move in, oh that was on October 31st, 1970 Bovill was still a really young town then Yeah, they were just building a depot

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Well Mr. Parker had a men's store and I think there was a, some fellow had started to have a butcher shop here

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: And I don't know, it wasn't much of anything I guess I don't know whether there was a, somebody had a grocery store I guess, one of them, I don't know if it was when we came here though or not, they came later, some of them And
